elements.sent-2411-001: We present a finite volume scheme for modeling the diffusion of charged particles, specifically ions, in constrained geometries using a degenerate Poisson-Nernst-Planck system with size exclusion yielding cross-diffusion.
elements.sent-2411-002: Our method utilizes a two-point flux approximation and is part of the exponentially fitted scheme framework.
elements.sent-2411-003: The scheme is shown to be thermodynamically consistent, as it ensures the decay of some discrete version of the free energy.
elements.sent-2411-004: Classical numerical analysis results -- existence of discrete solution, convergence of the scheme as the grid size and the time step go to $0$ -- follow.
elements.sent-2411-005: We also investigate the long-time behavior of the scheme, both from a theoretical and numerical point of view.
elements.sent-2411-006: Numerical simulations confirm our findings, but also point out some possibly very slow convergence towards equilibrium of the system under consideration.
elements.sent-2411-007: Motivated by the transfer of ions in confined geometries, Burger {\em et al. introduced in \cite{BDPS10 a model accounting for cross-diffusion and size-exclusion effects.}}
elements.sent-2411-008: The model \cite{BSW12 further incorporated self-consistent electric interaction.}
elements.sent-2411-009: In this model, $I$ species, the volume fractions of which being denoted by $U = (u_i)_{1\leq i\leq I}$, are subject to diffusion as well as to electric forces induced by a self-consistent electrostatic potential.
elements.sent-2411-010: Denote by $\O\subset \RR^d$ a bounded connected polyhedral domain, then the conservation of the volume occupied by the species $i$ writes \begin{equation}\label{eq:contmodel_conserv} \partial_t u_i + \diver F_i = 0, \qquad i =1,\dots,I,\end{equation} with the flux of the species $i$ being (formally) given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:Fi} F_i = - D_i \left(u_0 \grad u_i - u_i \grad u_0 + u_0u_i z_i \grad \phi\right) = - D_i u_i u_0 \nabla\left(\log\left(\frac{u_i}{u_0}\right)+ z_i\phi\right).\end{equation}.
